summary: The divide between development and data teams remains a persistent problem, with devs often unaware of or indifferent to data teams' needs. Data teams depend on dev for their data sources, but without clear communication and incentives, dev teams ignore their requests. Reis urges data teams to proactively build bridges through communication and education to foster empathy.
key_points: Devs are largely unaware of data teams' needs and lack incentive to help them
Data is increasingly integrated into applications rather than living outside them
Communication and collaboration are the primary solutions to bridge the divide
Simple actions like lunch-and-learns can build empathy between teams
The software and data divide will eventually shrink as data becomes the application
money_quotes: One dev put it perfectly - he'd probably help the data people at his company if 1) the data people were crystal clear on what they wanted from him and 2) he had the incentive and bandwidth to help. But since he had no time or incentive to help anyone but his immediate team, the requests from the data team were ignored.

I urge data teams to build a bridge with the dev teams they depend upon. It doesn't need to be fancy. Do lunch and learns with the dev team. Educate them on what you do, and why it matters to the company. Simple things like this will build empathy, and a little empathy goes a long ways.
The world has moved on from data teams providing basic reporting. It did that a while ago.
key_insight: Data teams must proactively communicate their value and needs to dev teams because devs won't prioritize what they don't understand or aren't incentivized to care about.
